content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 29, 1988


Judith Harper
Kronick, Moskovitz, Tiedemann & Girard
770 L Street, Suite 1200
Sacramento, CA  95814-3363

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. 88-333

Dear Ms. Harper:

     Your letter requesting advice concerning the recently
enacted campaign finance reform initiatives (Government Code
Section 85100, et seq.) was received on August 29, 1988 by the
Fair Political Practices Commission. If you have any questions
about your advice request, you may contact John McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

     Because of the delayed operative date of most of the
provisions of these initiatives, and the numerous inquiries we
are receiving relative to implementation, we are attempting to
respond to questions regarding interpretation in an organized
and efficient fashion. Due to the volume of requests and
complexity of the issues involved, we will answer your question
in a timely manner, but not within the usual twenty-one working
days. (Government Code Section 83114(b).)

     If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed. You also should be aware
that your letter and our response are public records which may
be disclosed to the public upon receipt of a proper request for
disclosure.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [illegible]
                                        Gregory W. Baughn
                                        Executive Director

GWB:plh:2173LTR


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                  Date : October 26, 1988


From : Fair Political Practices Commission
       John G. McLean

Subject :

       T-88-333


       Spoke today with Judy Harper. Informed her that we had
written a letter to the Southern California Association of
Governments (SCAG) which I felt would answer her questions:
Specifically, they can send mass mailings to other government
agencies in the normal course of business without encountering
the mass mailing prohibition. The prohibition applies to mass
mailings which include references to members of JPAs who are
elected officials in some other capacity.

JGM:ld:T88333

ADOLPH MOSKOVITZ

A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

ATTORNEYS AT LAW
400 CAPITOL MALL, SUITE 1200
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814-3363

TELEPHONE (916) 444-6920
TELECOPIER (916) 444-5618

August 25, 1988

OF COUNSEL
E. LEONARD MEISELMAN (1913-1987)
LEONARD M. FRIEDMAN
WILLIAM E. BYRNE

Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento 95814

RE: JPA Mass Mailings under Proposition 73

Dear Friends:

A question has arisen with one of our clients over the
mass mailing provisions of Proposition 73. Looking at the
emergency regulations you adopted in July, the answer seems
relatively clear. However, since the whole subject area is
somewhat murky, I would ask for a more formal opinion from FPPC.

The California Association of Joint Powers Authorities
(CAJPA) is a statewide association of more than 50 joint powers
authorities that consist of several thousand various local
governments, school districts, water districts, fire districts
and other special districts. Most of these JPAs pool their
resources for insurance purposes.

The question that they have raised is whether
communication with members of a joint powers authority (JPA)
would constitute a mass mailing, since their governing boards may
conceivably contain elected officials.

An example is the Schools Excess Liability Fund (SELF),
with 993 member agencies. While they are not a political entity
in any way, some of their governing board members are school
board members from various jurisdictions around the state, and
thus are elected officials.

Fair Political Practices Commission        99.92-LT-082588-439
August 25, 1988
Page 2


     My reading of the situation is that the mailings of
JPAs such as SELF to their members fit under the exception
(c)(2), "mailings sent in the normal course of business from one
governmental entity or officer to another. . ." But what about
mailings sent to suppliers, or to professional or business
contacts for example?

     An additional factor that I think would be considered
is that the elected officials in this instance were not elected
to the JPA board, but to some member agency within the JPA.
Would that make a difference?

     If you have further questions, please don't hesitate to
contact me directly. Thank you for your attention.

                                          Very truly yours,

                                          [signature]
                                          JUDITH A. HARPER

/JAH

cc:     John Wilson




KRONICK, MOSKOWITZ,
TIEDEMANN & GIRARD
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

embedding.qa_text: This letter is from the California Fair Political Practices Commission, dated August 29, 1988. It responds to a request for advice regarding recently enacted campaign finance reform initiatives (Government Code Section 85100, et seq.). The Commission informs Ms. Harper that their response will be provided in a timely manner but not within the usual twenty-one working days due to the volume of requests and complexity of the issues involved. If more information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response will contact her shortly. The Commission also notes that both the letter and its response are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon receipt of a proper request for disclosure. The letter is signed by Gregory W. The prohibition applies to mass mailings which include references to members of JPAs who are elected officials in some other capacity. It is addressed to the Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices Commission in Sacramento. The letter discusses a question regarding mass mailings under Proposition 73 and involves the California Association of Joint Powers Authorities (CAJPA). The CAJPA consists of more than 50 joint powers authorities that pool their resources for insurance purposes. The question raised is whether communication with members of a joint powers authority (JPA) would constitute a mass mailing, since their governing boards may contain elected officials. An example given is the Schools Excess Liability Fund (SELF), which has 993 member agencies, some of whose governing board members are school board members from various jurisdictions around the state, and thus are elected officials. My reading of the situation is that the mailings of JPAs such as SELF to their members fit under the exception (c)(2), "mailings sent in the normal course of business from one governmental entity or officer to another..." But what about mailings sent to suppliers, or to professional or business contacts for example? An additional factor that I think would be considered is that the elected officials in this instance were not elected to the JPA board, but to some member agency within the JPA. Would that make a difference? If you have further questions, please don't hesitate to contact me directly. Thank you for your attention. Very truly yours, JUDITH A.
